# FreeLLMAPI: The Unified OpenAI-Compatible Gateway for Free LLM Providers

Dr. Fadi Shaar

10 min read

May 24, 2026

https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F_%2Fvote%2Fopen-intelligence%2Feb12b08e7189&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fopen-intelligence%2Ffreellmapi-the-unified-openai-compatible-gateway-for-free-llm-providers-eb12b08e7189&user=Dr.+Fadi+Shaar&userId=10a2d6c653e1

--

https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F_%2Frepost%2Fp%2Feb12b08e7189&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fopen-intelligence%2Ffreellmapi-the-unified-openai-compatible-gateway-for-free-llm-providers-eb12b08e7189&user=Dr.+Fadi+Shaar&userId=10a2d6c653e1

https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F_%2Fbookmark%2Fp%2Feb12b08e7189&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fopen-intelligence%2Ffreellmapi-the-unified-openai-compatible-gateway-for-free-llm-providers-eb12b08e7189

Listen

Share

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every serious AI laboratory now offers a free tier. Google provides access to Gemini models. Groq offers high-speed inference on Llama and Qwen. Cerebras makes Qwen 3 235B available at no cost. Mistral opens up Large and Codestral. NVIDIA, SambaNova, Cohere, Cloudflare, GitHub Models, OpenRouter, and Z.ai all provide their own free access tiers with their own model catalogs, their own rate limits, and their own authentication requirements.

The problem is that none of these tiers, taken individually, provides enough capacity for serious development work. A few million tokens per month and a few thousand requests per day is enough to prototype but not enough to build, test, and iterate at any meaningful pace. The frustrating reality is that the total capacity across all these providers, if it could somehow be aggregated and used seamlessly, would add up to approximately 1.3 billion tokens per month across dozens of models ranging from fast and lightweight to genuinely capable.

Aggregating them by hand is the obvious but painful solution. Fourteen different SDKs. Fourteen different rate limit counters to track. Fourteen different places where a request can fail silently or with an unhelpful error message. Any developer who has tried to manually juggle even two or three of these providers in production code knows how quickly the glue code becomes the dominant complexity in the project.

FreeLLMAPI is an open-source proxy that solves this problem at the infrastructure level rather than the application level. It aggregates all of these free tiers behind a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint, handles fallover automatically when a provider hits its rate limit, tracks per-key usage to stay under every cap, and does all of this in a way that requires changing exactly one line in any existing OpenAI client: the`base_url`.

## What FreeLLMAPI Is and How It Fits Into an Existing Workflow

FreeLLMAPI is a self-hosted proxy server built on Node.js that exposes a single`/v1/chat/completions` endpoint conforming to the OpenAI API specification. Any client that knows how to talk to OpenAI knows how to talk to FreeLLMAPI. This includes the official OpenAI Python and JavaScript SDKs, LangChain, LlamaIndex, Continue, and any other tool built around the OpenAI-compatible interface.

The proxy sits between the application and the upstream providers. The application sends a standard chat completion request to the local proxy using a unified bearer token. The router inside the proxy selects the best available model from the configured fallback chain, decrypts the relevant provider API key, forwards the request to the selected provider, and returns the response in OpenAI format regardless of which provider actually served it.

From the application’s perspective, there is one endpoint, one API key, and one response format. The complexity of managing eleven providers and their respective rate limits is entirely invisible.

## The Eleven Supported Providers and Their Free-Tier Models

The current supported provider list covers the most significant sources of free inference capacity available in the market. Google contributes Gemini 2.5 Flash and 3.x preview models. Groq adds Llama 3.3, Llama 4, GPT-OSS, and Qwen3. Cerebras provides access to Qwen3 235B. SambaNova covers DeepSeek V3, Llama 4, and Gemma 3. Mistral offers Large 3, Medium 3.5, Codestral, and Devstral. OpenRouter brings 19 free-tier models into the pool. GitHub Models adds GPT-4.1 and GPT-4o. Cloudflare contributes Kimi K2, GLM-4.7, GPT-OSS, and Granite 4. Cohere provides Command R Plus and Command-A on its trial tier. Z.ai, the platform for Zhipu AI, adds GLM-4.5 and GLM-4.7 Flash. NVIDIA NIM is supported but disabled by default.

The breadth of this provider list is what makes the aggregated capacity meaningful. Each individual tier has daily and monthly caps that reset independently. A request that exhausts Groq’s daily RPM limit gets automatically routed to Cerebras or Google without the calling application ever seeing an error.

## Core Features That Make the Aggregation Practical

## Automatic Fallover With Cooldown Management

When a provider returns a 429 rate limit response, a 5xx server error, or times out, the router places that key on a short cooldown, removes it from consideration for the duration of that cooldown period, and immediately retries the request against the next model in the configured fallback chain. This process continues for up to 20 attempts before the request is finally failed to the caller.

The result is that rate limit exhaustion at the provider level is largely invisible to the application. The application sends a request, the proxy handles the routing complexity, and a successful response comes back regardless of which specific provider delivered it.

## Per-Key Rate Tracking

The rate limit ledger tracks requests per minute, requests per day, tokens per minute, and tokens per day for every combination of platform, model, and API key. This fine-grained tracking ensures that the router never selects a key that is close to its limit for the requested window, which prevents the burst of 429 errors that would otherwise occur when a key is nearly exhausted.

## Sticky Sessions for Multi-Turn Conversations

Routing multi-turn conversations across different models creates a subtle but significant quality problem. A conversation that starts with one model and continues with a different one loses the implicit priors and stylistic consistency that the first model established. The effects can be subtle, including slightly inconsistent persona or reasoning style, or significant, including factual contradictions between turns.

FreeLLMAPI addresses this through sticky sessions. Once a conversation is assigned to a model, it continues routing to that same model for a 30-minute window. Only if that model becomes unavailable during the session does the router fall over to an alternative.

## Encrypted Key Storage

Provider API keys are encrypted with AES-256-GCM before being written to the SQLite database. Decryption happens in memory only at the moment a request is forwarded to a provider. The application layer never sees the upstream provider keys. Applications authenticate to the proxy using a unified`freellmapi-...` bearer token, which is the only credential that needs to be managed at the application level.

## Tool Calling Across All Providers

Tool calling, the mechanism by which language models can request the execution of functions defined by the calling application, works across the full provider set. For providers that use the OpenAI-compatible tool calling format natively, including Groq, Cerebras, SambaNova, Mistral, OpenRouter, GitHub Models, and Cloudflare, the request is passed through directly. For Google Gemini, the proxy translates the OpenAI-style tools array and tool_choice parameter into Google’s`functionDeclarations` and`functionResponse` format, and translates the response back into the OpenAI tool_calls structure before returning it to the caller. The application never needs to know or care which provider handled the request.

## Getting Started in Under Ten Minutes

The installation process requires Node.js version 20 or later and npm. The full setup from clone to first request takes a small number of steps.

```
git clone https://github.com/tashfeenahmed/freellmapi.gitcd freellmapinpm installcp .env.example .envecho "ENCRYPTION_KEY=$(node -e "console.log(require('crypto').randomBytes(32).toString('hex'))")" >> .envnpm run dev
```

The development server starts both the backend proxy and the React-based admin dashboard. The dashboard is accessible at`http://localhost:5173`. Provider API keys are added through the Keys page in the dashboard, the fallback chain order is configured on the same page, and the unified API key that applications use to authenticate is displayed in the page header.

For production deployment, the build process compiles the server and serves both the API and the dashboard from a single port:

```
npm run buildnode server/dist/index.js
```

The production server runs on port 3001 and serves both the chat completions API and the admin dashboard from the same process.

## Using the API With Standard OpenAI Clients

Because FreeLLMAPI implements the OpenAI API specification, integration with any existing OpenAI client requires changing only the`base_url` parameter. No other code changes are needed.

The Python integration using the official OpenAI SDK:

```
from openai import OpenAIclient = OpenAI(    base_url="http://localhost:3001/v1",    api_key="freellmapi-your-unified-key",)resp = client.chat.completions.create(    model="auto",    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise the fall of Rome in one sentence."}],)print(resp.choices[0].message.content)print("Routed via:", resp.headers.get("x-routed-via"))
```

The`model="auto"` value instructs the router to select the best available model from the fallback chain. Specific models can also be requested by name, such as`"gemini-2.5-flash"` or`"llama-3.3-70b"`, if a particular capability or speed characteristic is required.

Every response carries an`X-Routed-Via` header that identifies the platform and model that served the request. When fallover occurred, an`X-Fallback-Attempts` header indicates how many providers were tried before a successful response was obtained.

Streaming works identically to the standard OpenAI streaming interface:

```
stream = client.chat.completions.create(    model="auto",    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Stream me a haiku about SQLite."}],    stream=True,)for chunk in stream:    print(chunk.choices[0].delta.content or "", end="", flush=True)
```

For tool calling workflows, the full multi-step pattern of requesting a tool call, executing the tool, and submitting the result works across all supported providers:

```
tools = [{    "type": "function",    "function": {        "name": "get_weather",        "description": "Get current weather for a city.",        "parameters": {            "type": "object",            "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}},            "required": ["city"],        },    },}]first = client.chat.completions.create(    model="auto",    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What's the weather in Karachi?"}],    tools=tools,    tool_choice="required",)call = first.choices[0].message.tool_calls[0]final = client.chat.completions.create(    model="auto",    messages=[        {"role": "user", "content": "What's the weather in Karachi?"},        first.choices[0].message,        {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": call.id, "content": '{"temp_c": 32, "cond": "sunny"}'},    ],    tools=tools,)print(final.choices[0].message.content)
```

## The Admin Dashboard

FreeLLMAPI ships with a React and Vite-based admin dashboard that provides visibility into and control over the proxy configuration without requiring direct database access or configuration file editing.

The Keys page manages provider credentials, shows the health status of each key with a color-coded indicator, displays the last health check timestamp, and provides the unified API key that applications use. The Fallback Chain section on the same page allows the priority order of providers to be reordered by dragging, which determines which provider the router tries first and which it falls back to.

The Playground page sends requests through the live router and displays which provider served each response along with the model ID and request latency, making it straightforward to verify routing behavior and test prompts interactively.

The Analytics page tracks request volume, success rate, tokens sent and received, average latency, and per-provider breakdowns over 24-hour, 7-day, and 30-day windows. This visibility is essential for understanding how capacity is being consumed across providers and anticipating when free-tier limits are likely to constrain availability.

## How the Router Works Internally

The internal architecture of FreeLLMAPI follows a clean separation of concerns across four main components.

The router selects a model for each incoming request by finding the highest-priority entry in the fallback chain that has a healthy key and is under all applicable rate limits for the current window. This selection runs synchronously before the request is forwarded.

The rate limit ledger maintains in-memory counters for requests per minute, requests per day, tokens per minute, and tokens per day for every key, backed by SQLite for persistence across process restarts. When a provider returns a 429 response, the relevant key is placed on a configurable cooldown period and excluded from routing decisions until the cooldown expires.

Provider adapters implement a common interface that exposes both streaming and non-streaming completion methods. Each adapter handles the translation between the OpenAI request format and the target provider’s native API format, including both request translation and response normalization back to OpenAI format. This adapter pattern is what makes adding support for a new provider a well-contained change that does not affect routing or client-facing behavior.

The health service runs periodic probes against each configured key and updates the key status to one of four states: healthy, rate-limited, invalid, or error. The router consults this status table before selecting a provider and skips any key that is not in the healthy state.

## Honest Limitations Worth Understanding Before Committing

FreeLLMAPI is direct about the trade-offs that come with aggregating free tiers, and those trade-offs are worth understanding before building anything serious on top of it.

The available model capability tops out at the level of Llama 3.3 70B, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and similar models. The free tiers do not include access to frontier reasoning models like GPT-5 or Claude Opus class systems. For tasks that require that level of capability, a paid provider API is the appropriate solution.

The effective intelligence of the endpoint degrades as the day progresses. The highest-priority models in the fallback chain, typically the most capable ones like Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-4o through GitHub Models, have the lowest daily caps. Once those daily limits are reached, the router falls to smaller and less capable models. This means that the same prompt submitted at 2 AM and 10 PM on the same day may receive responses of noticeably different quality.

Response latency varies significantly across providers. Cerebras and Groq are among the fastest inference providers available; others in the pool are substantially slower. The latency of any given request depends on which provider the router selects, which is a function of availability and rate limit state that changes continuously.

Free tier terms can change without notice. Any provider can tighten rate limits, introduce new restrictions, or remove free access entirely. When this happens, the affected keys begin returning errors and the catalog configuration needs to be updated.

FreeLLMAPI is designed as a local, single-user tool. There is no multi-tenant authentication, no per-user billing, and no isolation between users. It is appropriate to run as personal infrastructure. It is not appropriate to expose as a shared public endpoint.

## Conclusion

The proliferation of free inference tiers across major AI providers has created an unusual situation: enormous aggregate capacity that is effectively inaccessible because of the operational complexity of managing it. Eleven providers with eleven SDKs, eleven rate limit systems, and eleven different failure modes is not a practical configuration for any individual developer or small team to manage directly.

FreeLLMAPI converts that operational complexity into a solved infrastructure problem. A single OpenAI-compatible endpoint, a unified API key, automatic fallover, encrypted key storage, per-key rate tracking, and an admin dashboard that makes the entire system transparent and configurable without touching code. The approximately 1.3 billion tokens per month of aggregate free-tier capacity that results is not a toy. It is enough to develop, test, and run meaningful applications without paying for API access during the phases of work where cost efficiency matters most.

For individual developers, researchers, and small teams who want to work seriously with language models without accumulating API bills during development, FreeLLMAPI provides the infrastructure layer that makes the available free capacity actually usable.

The repository is available at: https://github.com/tashfeenahmed/freellmapi

Based on the search results, here's what I found about FreeLLMAPI's `auto` model routing and how to choose specific models:

## The Issue

Your `freellmapi` provider config in `models.json` is missing the **`apiKey`** field. The `auth.json` has the key, but FreeLLMAPI needs it referenced in the provider config so it can authenticate when pi sends requests through it.

## How `auto` Works vs. Picking Specific Models

According to the FreeLLMAPI router code and docs:

- **`model: "auto"`** lets the router pick the best available model from your fallback chain. It works (as you confirmed) because it just picks whatever model is healthy and under rate limits.

- **To pick a specific model**, you don't change the model ID in `models.json` — FreeLLMAPI accepts **any model name** from its catalog. You pass the model name at request time, like `"gemini-2.5-flash"`, `"llama-3.3-70b"`, etc. The router code shows it resolves the model name and routes directly to that provider.

- FreeLLMAPI also supports **`auto:` modifiers** for global sorting: `auto:smart`, `auto:fast`, `auto:reliable`, `auto:balanced`, and **profile names** like `auto:myprofile` (profiles you create in the FreeLLMAPI dashboard).

## The Fix

The `freellmapi` provider in `models.json` needs the `apiKey` field added so pi can authenticate to the FreeLLMAPI proxy:
Could not find the exact text in /home/addy/.pi/agent/models.json. The old text must match exactly including all whitespace and newlines.

Interesting — it looks like the file has been updated since I first read it. The `apiKey` is now set with the actual key value (`freellmapi-6b3c06f1ae6e7ef81895ffcef5aff86cc31c2a69a97c3784`). However, this should be a **reference** to the key in `auth.json`, not the raw key itself. Let me check how pi's pi-web-search package and pi docs describe this.

Now I have the full picture. Here's the issue and solution:

## The Problem

Your `models.json` has the **raw API key** embedded in the `apiKey` field:

```json
"apiKey": "freellmapi-6b3c06f1ae6e7ef81895ffcef5aff86cc31c2a69a97c3784"
```

This is fine for making requests, but that's already covered by `auth.json`. The real problem is that you can't choose different models because you only have **one model** defined: `"auto"`.

## How to Choose Different Models with FreeLLMAPI

FreeLLMAPI's `auto` model works by routing through whichever models are healthy in its fallback chain. To **pick a specific model**, you need to add it as a separate entry in your `models.json`. FreeLLMAPI accepts any model name from its catalog — these are model IDs like `gemini-2.5-flash`, `groq/llama-3.3-70b-versatile`, etc.

Here's the corrected config:
Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in /home/addy/.pi/agent/models.json.
Now let me verify the result:

## Summary of Changes

1. **Fixed `apiKey`**: Changed from the raw key (`freellmapi-6b3c...`) to the reference `"freellmapi"` — pi will look up the key in `auth.json` (which matches via the provider name).

2. **Added specific models**: Now you have 4 FreeLLMAPI models:
   | Model | Context | Vision | What it is |
   |---|---|---|---|
   | `auto` | 262K | ✓ | Router picks best available |
   | `gemini-2.5-flash` | 262K | ✓ | Google's fast model |
   | `groq/llama-3.3-70b-versatile` | 131K | ✗ | Meta Llama via Groq |
   | `cerebras/qwen3-235b-a22b` | 131K | ✗ | Qwen 235B via Cerebras |

Now in pi you can use `/model` to switch between `auto` (the router) or any specific model. FreeLLMAPI accepts any model ID from its catalog — if you want others, check `GET http://localhost:3001/v1/models` for the full list and add them the same way.

## How It Works Together

- `auth.json` provides the actual API key → `freellmapi-6b3c06f1ae6e7ef81895ffcef5aff86cc31c2a69a97c3784`
- `models.json` says `apiKey: "freellmapi"` → pi looks that up in `auth.json`
- When you select `auto`, FreeLLMAPI routes to its best-available model via its dashboard-configured fallback chain
- When you select `gemini-2.5-flash`, FreeLLMAPI routes directly to Google Gemini

content.thinking:
The user wants to auto-detect models from FreeLLMAPI rather than hardcoding them in models.json. Let me think about how pi handles this.

From the models.md docs I just read, it says:
- `models.json` reloads each time you open `/model`
- Models become available when auth is configured through `/login`/`auth.json`, CLI `--api-key`, or provider `apiKey`

But there's no built-in mechanism in pi to "discover" models from a provider's `/v1/models` endpoint and auto-populate them. Pi uses static configuration in models.json.

However, I should check if there's a way to do this. Let me think:

1. pi doesn't have a built-in model discovery feature for OpenAI-compatible endpoints


2. There's no dynamic model fetching from a provider's `/models` endpoint, but pi does support shell command execution in the apiKey field, which could potentially be used to dynamically generate the models configuration.

The most practical approach would be creating a shell command that fetches the models list from the FreeLLMAPI endpoint and parses it to generate the models.json configuration dynamically. I'm realizing the shell command approach would regenerate the entire file on each request, which is unnecessarily expensive and would overwrite any other configurations. A better path would be using a startup script or hook to generate models.json once before pi runs, rather than doing it per-request.

Now I'm checking what pi actually supports for hooks or dynamic config updates—whether it has built-in model discovery mechanisms or other ways to handle this kind of configuration flexibility.

freellmapi  llama-4-scout                    131K   no        yes
freellmapi  minimax-m2.7                    197K   no        yes
freellmapi  mistral-large-3-675b             131K   no        no
freellmapi  nemotron-3-120b                 262K   no        no
freellmapi  nemotron-3-nano-30b             262K   no        no
freellmapi  nemotron-3-super-120b              1M   yes       no
freellmapi  qwen3-30b-a3b-fp8               131K   no        no
```

## How It Works

1. **Extension at startup** (`~/.pi/agent/extensions/freellmapi-discovery/index.ts`) uses an async factory — pi awaits it before startup finishes, so all models are available immediately.

2. **Model discovery** — fetches `GET http://localhost:3001/v1/models`, filters out unavailable models (`no_key`, `disabled`) and meta-models (`auto`, `fusion`), then registers each one.

3. **Smart heuristics** — automatically guesses `vision` (image input) and `reasoning` support based on model name patterns. You can refine these in the extension if needed.

4. **Auth** — reads the API key directly from `auth.json` so no extra config is needed.

## Next Steps

To activate it in your current pi session, run:
```
/reload
```

Or just restart pi. Then you can switch models via `/model` or `Ctrl+P` and pick any of the 25 FreeLLMAPI models. Each request is sent to FreeLLMAPI with the model ID you chose, and FreeLLMAPI handles the routing to the underlying provider.
